reasoning: The Lean statement encodes the informal Putnam 1998 B6 using `Real.sqrt` and asks whether its value equals some integer (via coercion). The universal quantification over a, b, c : ℤ, the existential over positive n : ℤ, and the cubic expression n³+an²+bn+c all match exactly. The predicate `¬∃ k : ℤ, (k:ℝ) = Real.sqrt(P(n))` is the standard Mathlib way to express "√P(n) is not an integer" — for P(n) > 0 it captures exactly "P(n) is not a perfect square," which is the intended meaning in the Putnam context. There is a minor quirk: Real.sqrt(negative) = 0 in Mathlib, so n with P(n) < 0 would not satisfy the Lean predicate. However, since P is a cubic with positive leading coefficient, witnesses with P(n) > 0 non-square always exist (the content of the Putnam theorem), so both statements are true with the same natural witnesses. The standard olympiad reading of "√N is not an integer" is "N is not a perfect square," which the Lean encoding captures correctly.
